text.title: xUnit Testing Failure Modes
text.summary: Four antipatterns observed in xUnit test suites. Each pairs symptom + root cause + fix.

antipattern.symptom: `async void` test methods that throw exceptions silently pass; CI green, prod broken.
antipattern.root-cause: Test method declared `async void`; xUnit runner cannot observe exceptions on non-awaitable signatures.
antipattern.fix: Change every async test signature to `public async Task ...`. Add a Roslyn analyzer (`xUnit1031`) at warning-as-error.

antipattern.symptom: Tests pass in isolation, fail when run in parallel. Shared `static Faker`, static caches, or mutable singletons collide across xUnit threads.

root-cause.source: xUnit docs — Running tests in parallel

antipattern.root-cause: Author treated test class as a process-singleton; xUnit defaults parallelize at class boundary.
antipattern.fix: Remove shared mutable state. Use `IClassFixture` for per-class setup. If tests truly must serialize, use `[CollectionDefinition(DisableParallelization = true)]`.

antipattern.symptom: `WebApplicationFactory<Program>` fails to compile with "Program class was not found" or "Entry point not found" after .NET 6 top-level statements.

root-cause.source: ASP.NET Core docs — integration tests with top-level statements

antipattern.root-cause: Top-level `Program.cs` declares `Program` as `internal`; test assembly cannot see it.
antipattern.fix: Add `public partial class Program { }` at the bottom of `Program.cs`. Restart test discovery.

antipattern.symptom: Integration tests use `UseInMemoryDatabase` but the code under test relies on relational constraints (FK cascade, unique index, transactions). Tests pass; production violates the constraint.

root-cause.source: EF Core docs — In-memory provider limitations

antipattern.root-cause: In-memory provider doesn't enforce relational invariants; the test is not exercising the actual database engine behaviour.
antipattern.fix: Switch to SQLite-in-memory (`Microsoft.Data.Sqlite` + `DbConnection.Open()`) for low-fidelity, or Testcontainers + the real DB engine for high-fidelity.
